definitions.definition: A solid in which the atoms, molecules or ions are arranged in a regular three-dimensional pattern. This regular arrangement of particles is called the crystal lattice.
A solid in which the atoms, molecules or ions are not arranged in a regular three-dimensional pattern.
The empty spaces or voids present between the atoms or molecules in the crystal lattice.
A small amount of a substance that is added to a material to alter its properties.
The substance in which the impurity is added.
An impurity in which the host atoms are replaced by the impurity atoms.
An impurity in which the impurity atoms occupy the interstitial spaces in the crystal lattice.
A defect in which a single atom, ion or molecule is missing from its normal position in the crystal lattice.
A type of point defect in which a pair of adjacent atoms are missing from the crystal lattice.
A type of point defect in which an atom or ion is missing from its normal position and is present in an interstitial space.
A line defect in which the atoms or molecules in the crystal lattice are displaced from their normal positions.
A type of dislocation in which the atoms or molecules in the crystal lattice are displaced from their normal positions along an edge of the crystal lattice.
A type of dislocation in which the atoms or molecules in the crystal lattice are displaced from their normal positions along a screw-like path.
A regular three-dimensional arrangement of atoms, molecules or ions in a solid.
The smallest group of atoms, molecules or ions that can be repeated to form the crystal lattice.
A three-dimensional arrangement of unit cells in a crystal lattice.
